retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Crown Adaptation
retrieved_docs.content: Operators can use the number 137 pliers can be used for circumferential contouring. Contouring pliers deliver a belling effect in the middle third of the crown. [19] Johnson contouring pliers with a No. 114 are used for cervical crown adaptation on the cervical third of the buccal and lingual surfaces. A curved beak plier No. 114 is then used to contour the buccal and lingual surfaces. Curved beak pliers may also contour the crown's proximal areas. [20] [13] Lastly, the crimping pliers are used to crimp the cervical margin 1 mm circumferentially for final adaptation and ensure that the crown margin's outline follows the gingival margin of the tooth. The operators can check the final adaptation of the crown by taking a radiograph to verify the gingival contour and extension, ensuring the complete coverage of the tooth. [13] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Buccal/Lingual Reduction
retrieved_docs.content: Reducing the buccal and lingual surfaces is the third step in the preparation. The reduction of the buccal and lingual mucosa is optional. Some authors suggested preparing the buccal and lingual walls to create a gingivally inclined long bevel to make crown placement easier. On the other hand, others advocated for none to minimal (0.5 to 1 mm) preparation on the vestibular and lingual aspect of the crown, unless pronounced enamel convexity should only be reduced by a small amount. [12] Duggal and Curzon recommended trying the selected crown before lingual and buccal reduction. [13] Lastly, the operator should round all line and point angles. Occlusal-lingual and occlusal-buccal angles are beveled at 45 degrees. Bur is held parallel to the tooth’s long axis, and one should carefully blend the surfaces. Remove any remaining caries and perform appropriate pulp therapy if pulp involvement is present. The rubber dam should be left in place until cementing the crown. If it interferes with the seating and adaptation of the crown, it can be removed after tooth preparation. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Crown Extension for Deep Subgingival Caries
retrieved_docs.content: Teeth with caries extending apically to the crown margin should have these areas filled with restoration before preparing the tooth to allow an appropriate finishing line for the crown, or a stainless steel extension could be soldered to the crown to form a flange for this area. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Oversized Crown
retrieved_docs.content: To minimize the crown diameter, the metal edges of the best-sized crown can be given a V cut on the buccal surface. The cut edges are then reapproximated. The overlapping margins are then welded together. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Complications -- Interproximal Ledge
retrieved_docs.content: Wrongly positioning the bur can result in a ledge instead of the desired shoulder-free interproximal cutting. The crown seating may be impeded if the operator fails to remove the ledge. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Crown Adaptation
retrieved_docs.content: Place the crown linguo-buccally on the prepared tooth, applying pressure in the buccal direction so the crown slips over the buccal surface and into the gingival sulcus. There is friction as the crown slides over the buccal bulge. Crown positioning be difficult if the crown is too small or if the buccal bulge is too excessive. Choose a larger crown or reduce the tooth's buccal bulge in this case. After placing the selected crown over the prepared tooth, ask the patient to bite over it. Check for preliminary occlusal and marginal ridge relation. Previously, scratch a mark on the stainless steel crown at the level of the free gingival margin. Scratch or dotted lines indicate the gingival contour and the portion of the crown to be removed. Remove the crown and trim or cut away the additional crown portion gingivally 1 mm below the scratch line with a crown and bridge scissors or, preferably, a large abrasive wheel (“heatless stone”) that allows for more precise reduction. Repeat the procedure until the adequate extension of 1 mm beneath the gingival margin is achieved.

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Undersized Crown
retrieved_docs.content: The crown wall of the best-fitting crown can be cut buccally or lingually, and an additional piece of 0.004-inch orthodontic stainless steel band can be welded over the gap that is then contoured to the desired form.

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Proximal Reduction
retrieved_docs.content: Proximal surfaces are reduced using a 69 L/ tapered fissure bur at high speed to make room for the crown and establish a feather finish line gingivally while avoiding damaging neighboring teeth. The mesial and distal contact points should be cleared so a probe could pass through them. Following the normal proximal contour, proximal slices should slightly converge toward the occlusal and lingual. A smooth taper from occlusal to gingival, free of ledges or shoulders, should be achieved. [10] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Indications -- Clinical Crown Lengthening
retrieved_docs.content: Primary indication: subgingival caries, coronal fracture, or restorative margin that cannot be restored with standard restorative procedures for tooth isolation and restoration. The tooth must be otherwise restorable. Teeth undergoing crown lengthening and adjacent teeth must meet the following requirements:

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- With Adjacent Stainless Steel Crowns
retrieved_docs.content: When more than 1 crown is required in a quadrant, both teeth should be prepared during the same visit. There should be slightly more than usual reduction on the adjacent proximal surfaces. This makes it easier to place multiple crowns. Before reducing another tooth, the occlusal reduction of one should be completed. Simultaneous reduction of both teeth results in improper reduction. There is a risk of mesiodistal space loss if there are proximal caries on adjacent teeth. To restore carious adjacent teeth with stainless steel crowns, the preparations should be modified to fit the teeth with smaller crowns than normal. Rather than more proximal reduction, further reduction of the buccal and lingual walls is carried out. [6] [10] [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Adjacent Stainless Steel Crown With Arch Length Loss/Space Loss
retrieved_docs.content: Reduction of the crown's mesiodistal dimension is frequently required, particularly when mesial drift (often caused by caries) has resulted in arch length loss. Flattening the crown's mesial and distal contact areas using Adam pliers results in a moderate reduction in the mesiodistal dimension. A contralateral upper tooth crown with a smaller size can be used if there is mesial drift in the lower arch. Another form of modification is vertical slicing and spot-welding, an additional segment of a stainless steel band. This is done to increase the perimeter or extend the length. However, the efficacy of the latter modifications remains largely untested. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Procedure
retrieved_docs.content: Place orthodontic separators between the contact point of the primary molar for 4 or 5 days to create room for the crown. Select the smallest-sized crown covering all the cusps, approaching the contact points, and giving a 'spring back' effect. Adjustments of the crown are to be made using band-forming pliers if necessary. Cementation of the crown with glass ionomer cement. The tooth must be maintained in occlusion until the cement sets, followed by excess removal using floss. [25] [26] The Hall technique should be chosen as an alternative when the traditional approach with crown preparation is implausible. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Indications for the use of stainless steel crowns: Following pulp therapy Multisurface caries restorations Patients at high risk of caries Deciduous teeth with developmental defects, such as amelogenesis imperfecta, dentinogenesis imperfecta, enamel hypoplasia Teeth with extensive wear Where it is likely that the restoration may fail, eg, the proximal box formed is extended beyond the anatomic line angles. Fractured teeth Abutment for space maintainer [6] Patients who are unlikely to attend follow up appointments [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Cementation
retrieved_docs.content: A luting cement should be used to secure the crown. Once the crown is seated on the prepared tooth, it should be kept under pressure while the cement hardens. Manufacturers recommend glass ionomer, zinc polycarboxylate, and zinc phosphate cement but fluoride-leaching cement has additional advantages. Due to the clinical advantages, RMGIC is the preferred choice. [22] However, research suggests the type of cement used has little effect on retention, with the essential retentive components derived from proper crown contouring and crimping. Operators must pay deliberate attention to the removal of excess cement. This is normally accomplished by running a pointed instrument around the cemented crown's margins and sliding knotted dental floss buccolingually through the contact areas after the cement setting. Excess cement has been shown to affect gingival health. [8] [6]

Mode of supply: Available as light, medium, and heavy body consistencies. 2. Composition: The base contains polyether, fillers, and plasticizers. The accelerator contains alkyl aromatic sulfonates, inert oils, and plasticizers. 3. Setting reaction: When base and accelerator are mixed in equal proportions, polyether rubber is formed. 4. Indications Fixed prosthodontics (crown and bridgework, particularly for multiple preparations) Implants Border molding in complete denture fabrication Indirect inlay and onlay impressions It can be used for corrective impressions as it can be added to previously set material. 5. retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Preparation -- Selection of Crown
retrieved_docs.content: The operator can select the crown using different methods: Measuring the mesiodistal dimension of the tooth before tooth preparation Trial and error method Crown selection after tooth preparation

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.
